Q1: I organized my study routine around visual materials primarily—I'd create detailed diagrams, color-coded notes, and mind maps for every topic. I dedicated about 31 hours a week to studying, mostly in the evenings when my anxiety felt slightly more manageable. I kept a strict schedule written out on paper because looking at it helped me feel in control, even when everything inside felt chaotic. The routine was my anchor, honestly, even though following it sometimes felt exhausting.
Q2: I tried desperately to keep regular times, but my anxiety would sometimes derail everything. When deadlines approached, I'd feel this overwhelming fear that made me study even more intensely, sometimes losing sleep. The pattern varied because my stress levels varied—some weeks I could stick to my schedule, other weeks I'd be paralyzed by worry and then have to cram everything into fewer days.
Q3: Usually fear prompted me to log in—fear that I'd missed an announcement, fear that an assignment had changed, fear that everyone else knew something I didn't. I'd also log in when I needed to check specific deadlines or download lecture slides. It was rarely because I felt eager or excited; it was more about managing my constant worry about falling behind.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became comfortable or routine for me—it always felt like a source of stress. Every time I logged in, I'd scan for anything that might have changed or any bad news. I preferred to download what I needed quickly and then work offline where I felt safer and less overwhelmed by all the notifications and activity.
Q5: The lecture slides and video recordings were most useful because I'm a visual learner. I could pause videos, screenshot important diagrams, and incorporate them into my handwritten notes. Being able to see concepts laid out visually helped them stick in my mind much better than just reading text. Those resources let me learn at my own pace without the pressure of real-time interaction.
Q6: I mostly avoided the discussion forums—they terrified me. The idea of posting something and being judged by my peers made my heart race. I also didn't engage much with optional readings because I was already stretched thin trying to perfect the required materials. My anxiety made me prioritize what was absolutely necessary over anything extra.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assessments or if I could clearly see how it would help me understand a concept visually. I needed to feel like my effort would translate into concrete results. With my anxiety being so high, I couldn't afford to spend energy on things that felt ambiguous or uncertain in their value.
Q8: Optional activities felt like traps to me—like they might secretly be important but I couldn't handle adding more to my plate. Required activities had clear expectations, which my anxious brain actually preferred despite the pressure. With optional work, I'd spiral into worry about whether I should do it, whether others were doing it, whether I'd regret not doing it.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my real studying happened offline. I'd download all the slides and readings, print them out, and annotate them extensively by hand. I created elaborate visual study guides that I'd spread across my desk and review repeatedly. I also used textbooks from the library and watched supplementary YouTube videos to see concepts explained in different visual ways.
Q10: My most productive studying happened when I was sitting with my printed materials, creating color-coded summaries and diagrams with no screens involved. I'd spend hours connecting concepts visually on large sheets of paper, testing myself with flashcards I made by hand. None of that showed up anywhere digital, but it's how I actually learned and achieved my exam score.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd watch a video once online to make sure I had the right version, then I'd download it or take detailed screenshots and notes. After that, all my engagement with that material happened offline. I found the LMS interface anxiety-inducing, so minimizing my time there while maximizing my offline study time was my strategy.
Q12: There were definitely periods when my anxiety spiked so badly that even opening my laptop felt impossible. During those times, I'd feel scared of what I might find—a grade I didn't want to see, a discussion I should have participated in. My low resilience meant that any small setback could send me into a spiral where engaging with anything course-related felt overwhelming.
Q13: My high stress and anxiety absolutely affected how I used the LMS. When I was overwhelmed, I'd log in only for the bare minimum—grabbing files and checking deadlines. Fatigue from poor sleep made the bright screen unbearable sometimes. I'd often have to choose between engaging with the LMS or managing my mental state, and self-preservation usually won.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd have materials open but my mind would be racing with worry instead of actually processing the content. I'd scroll through readings while catastrophizing about exams or comparing myself to classmates. My eyes would move across the screen but nothing was sinking in because my anxiety had hijacked my attention completely.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts constantly because the fear of judgment was paralyzing. I knew participating mattered, but every time I tried to write something, I'd convince myself it wasn't good enough. I'd also delay checking grades because I was terrified of disappointment, even though logically I knew I was doing well. The avoidance was my anxiety's way of protecting me from perceived threats.
Q16: Deadlines were both my enemy and my motivator. They created intense fear that pushed me to complete everything on time—my 91.8% completion rate reflects that. But the days leading up to deadlines were torture, filled with panic and obsessive checking. I'd log in repeatedly near deadlines just to make sure I hadn't missed anything or made a submission error.
Q17: Feedback affected me deeply, sometimes too deeply. Positive feedback provided temporary relief from my constant worry. Negative or constructive feedback could send me into an anxiety spiral where I'd question everything I thought I knew. I'd often delay looking at feedback because I was scared of what it might say, even when I'd done well.
Q18: The course structure helped because it gave me clear expectations and a predictable schedule to follow. I appreciated knowing exactly what was required and when. However, the structure didn't account for students like me who struggle with the social aspects—the expectation of discussion participation felt like a constant source of dread that I had to force myself through.
Q19: Having materials available for download made engagement easier because I could study in my own safe space. The visual resources like diagrams and video lectures suited my learning style well. What made it harder was anything requiring real-time interaction or public participation—those elements clashed with my anxiety and made me want to withdraw from the course entirely.
Q20: I think some students might be frantically searching for reassurance or checking things repeatedly without actually being able to focus and produce work. High anxiety can make you obsessively engage with a platform without being productive. They might also be struggling to understand what's expected and clicking around trying to figure it out, or they could be dealing with perfectionism that prevents them from submitting anything.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. I achieved a distinction with a 99 on my exam, but my LMS engagement was low because my real learning happened offline. I downloaded materials once and then studied intensively from printed copies and handmade notes. The LMS was just a delivery system for me—my actual engagement with the content was deep but invisible to the system.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and whether they submitted work on time. But they completely miss the quality and depth of offline engagement—all my visual note-taking, my hours of creating study diagrams, my genuine understanding of the material. They also can't capture the emotional experience of learning, like how scared I felt every time I logged in.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely wrestling with the material until I understand it deeply, not just clicking through content. For me, it's about creating visual representations that make concepts stick, testing myself repeatedly, and feeling confident in my knowledge. It's definitely not about how many times I log in or how long I spend on a platform—it's about what's happening in my mind.
Q24: The LMS data would never show how much genuine learning happened at my desk with printed materials spread everywhere. It wouldn't show the hours I spent creating visual study aids or the depth of understanding I developed through offline review. It also wouldn't show how much my anxiety affected my experience—how logging in felt threatening, how I avoided discussions out of fear, or how my high performance came despite constant internal struggle rather than because everything was easy.
